Upon completion of the expansion, total Company headcount is projected to be 2,140 (current) + 420 (expansion) = **2,560 employees**, with a total projected annual payroll of \$112.4 million (current) + \$23.6 million (expansion) = **\$136.0 million**.

**[4. Payroll Vendor Readiness]{.underline}**

**[4.1 PinnacleHR Solutions Capabilities]{.underline}**

PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. (\"PinnacleHR\"), headquartered in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, has been Vantage\'s payroll processing vendor since 2018. Our account manager, Priya Samuelson, has been responsive and knowledgeable regarding the Company\'s payroll needs. PinnacleHR currently processes bi-weekly payroll for all 2,140 employees across New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut, including federal and state income tax withholding, FICA, and all applicable state-mandated deductions.

With respect to state-mandated paid leave programs, PinnacleHR currently withholds and remits employee contributions for the following programs in our existing three states:

> • **New Jersey:** Family Leave Insurance (NJ FLI) and Temporary Disability Insurance (NJ TDI)
>
> • **New York:** Paid Family Leave (NY PFL) and short-term disability insurance (NY DBL)
>
> • **Connecticut:** Connecticut Paid Leave (CT PL)

I have confirmed with Priya Samuelson that PinnacleHR has the technical capability to process payroll in all seven states (NJ, NY, CT, MA, CO, OR, and WA) and can handle withholding for the state-mandated paid leave programs in each of the four expansion states. PinnacleHR maintains current configurations for the Massachusetts Paid Family and Medical Leave program, the Colorado FAMLI program, Paid Leave Oregon, and the Washington PFML program, as PinnacleHR services other employer clients in those states.

However, there is a critical caveat. In an email dated approximately March 15, 2025, Priya Samuelson confirmed that employer-side registrations for state leave programs are the Company\'s responsibility and are not performed by PinnacleHR. PinnacleHR cannot initiate withholding or premium remittance for any state paid leave program until the employer has completed registration with the applicable state agency and has provided PinnacleHR with the registration confirmation and employer account numbers. Priya further stated that PinnacleHR\'s lead time for configuring withholding in a new state is approximately six to eight weeks from the date PinnacleHR receives the employer\'s registration confirmation and account credentials.

As of the date of this memorandum, no withholdings are configured for Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, or Washington. This is entirely a dependency on the Company completing its registration with each state\'s program first. PinnacleHR is prepared to act once we provide them with the necessary registration information, but we must take the first step.

**[4.2 Registration Action Items and Timeline Concerns]{.underline}**

Based on the opening dates for each expansion facility and PinnacleHR\'s stated six-to-eight-week configuration lead time, I have consolidated the registration urgency as follows:

**Colorado --- MOST URGENT.** Three remote employees are being hired in April 2025, before the Aurora facility opens in November 2025. If the Colorado FAMLI registration requirement is triggered by hiring the first employee who works in Colorado --- regardless of whether a physical facility exists --- then the Company may already be behind schedule, or will be within days. Even under the more favorable interpretation that registration is tied to the November 1, 2025 facility opening, the six-to-eight-week PinnacleHR configuration lead time means that registration would need to be completed by approximately mid-August 2025 at the latest. I need immediate guidance from Legal on the applicable trigger date and the steps required to register.

**Massachusetts --- Critical Path.** The Worcester facility opens August 1, 2025. Accounting for PinnacleHR\'s six-to-eight-week lead time, employer registration with the Massachusetts PFML program must be completed by approximately mid-May 2025. That is less than two months from the date of this memorandum. I label this item \"critical path\" because any delay in registration will cascade into a delay in PinnacleHR\'s configuration, which could result in the Company operating in Massachusetts without proper withholding in place on the facility\'s opening day.

**Oregon --- Moderate Urgency.** The Tigard facility opens February 1, 2026. Registration with Paid Leave Oregon should be completed by approximately October to November 2025 to allow PinnacleHR adequate configuration time.

**Washington --- Lower Urgency but Requires Planning.** The Kent facility opens May 1, 2026. Registration with Washington PFML should be completed by approximately January to February 2026.

I respectfully request that Legal --- specifically, Rebecca --- provide direction on: (a) which state agencies must be contacted for each registration; (b) what registration forms, employer identification numbers, or other documentation are required; (c) whether any of these registrations can or should be delegated to PinnacleHR or handled directly by outside counsel; and (d) whether the anticipated engagement with outside employment counsel can include assistance with these filings. Time is of the essence, particularly for Colorado and Massachusetts.

**[5. HR Infrastructure Gaps and Scalability Concerns]{.underline}**

**[5.1 Leave Tracking --- Current State and Challenges]{.underline}**

As described in Section 2.2 above, the Company currently tracks sick leave accruals, usage, and balances through a manual, spreadsheet-based system. This section provides a more detailed assessment of the system\'s deficiencies and explains why I believe it is inadequate for seven-state operations.

**Current System Description.** Two HR coordinators on my team --- Jessica Ferraro and Daniel Okafor --- are responsible for updating the leave tracking spreadsheets on a bi-weekly basis, timed to coincide with each payroll cycle. There is one Microsoft Excel workbook per state, for a current total of three workbooks (New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut). Each workbook contains columns for: employee name, employee ID, state, hire date, full-time or part-time status, hours worked in the applicable pay period, sick leave accrued in the period, sick leave used in the period, and a running balance of available sick leave hours. The system relies on basic Excel formulas for running totals but has no automated calculation logic for accrual rates, cap enforcement, waiting-period determinations, or carryover calculations. All of those determinations must be manually verified by Jessica and Daniel.

**Specific Deficiencies Identified:**

1\. **No state-specific rule differentiation.** The spreadsheets do not incorporate any mechanism to differentiate state-specific sick leave rules within a single workbook or to ensure consistency in how rules are applied across workbooks. Each state in which we operate has its own accrual rates, maximum accrual caps, waiting periods before employees can use accrued leave, carryover provisions at year-end, and benefit calculation formulas. Currently, the HR team applies what is effectively a single company-wide policy, but I am aware that state laws vary --- in some cases significantly --- and that our current approach may not satisfy each state\'s specific requirements. Expanding from three states to seven will compound this problem, as each new state brings its own set of rules that must be tracked individually.

2\. **No automated alerts.** The system provides no automated notifications when an employee reaches an accrual cap, becomes eligible to use accrued leave after the applicable waiting period, or approaches a carryover deadline at year-end. Jessica and Daniel must manually review each employee\'s record to identify these events. With 2,140 employees currently and 2,560 projected, this is not a scalable process.

3\. **No audit trail.** Changes made to the Excel spreadsheets are not logged or tracked. If a balance is corrected --- whether due to a data entry error, a retroactive adjustment, or a recalculation --- there is no record of the original entry, the date the change was made, or the reason for the correction. In the event of a regulatory audit or employee complaint, we would have no documentation to demonstrate how a particular balance was determined.

4\. **No integration with HRIS or time and attendance systems.** The leave tracking spreadsheets are standalone files. They are not linked to or integrated with WorkBridge Pro (our HRIS) or ClockSync Enterprise (our time and attendance system). Hours-worked data must be manually extracted from ClockSync reports and entered into the leave spreadsheets by hand. Similarly, employee demographic data such as hire dates, full-time/part-time status changes, and termination dates must be manually cross-referenced against WorkBridge Pro. This creates a meaningful risk of transcription errors, delays in updating records, and data inconsistencies between systems.

5\. **Limited scope.** The spreadsheet system tracks only earned sick leave. It does not track paid family leave or general PTO. Paid family leave requests are tracked through a separate paper-based process --- employees submit request forms that are approved by their supervisor and then filed in the employee\'s physical personnel folder in the HR office. PTO and vacation accruals are tracked within WorkBridge Pro but are not connected to the sick leave spreadsheets. This fragmented approach means there is no single system that provides a comprehensive view of an employee\'s total leave entitlements and usage.

6\. **Scaling concerns.** Expanding from three spreadsheets (for three states and 2,140 employees) to seven spreadsheets (for seven states and 2,560 employees) will increase the workload on Jessica and Daniel by an estimated 50% to 75%. This is not sustainable without either additional headcount or a technology investment, and likely both. The complexity increase is not merely linear --- each new state adds its own regulatory nuances that require the coordinators to learn and consistently apply new rules, increasing the cognitive burden and the probability of error.

**Recent Errors.** I want to note for the record that several errors have been identified during recent internal audits of the leave tracking spreadsheets. In one instance, a part-time warehouse associate in New Jersey had her sick leave balance capped at 24 hours despite having worked sufficient hours during the accrual period to accumulate a higher balance. The error was caused by the application of the wrong accrual rate for part-time employees, and the affected employee\'s balance had to be retroactively corrected. In a separate instance, a full-time employee at the Queens, New York facility was told by his supervisor that he could not use accrued sick leave until 120 days after his hire date. When the employee inquired with HR, we confirmed that the 120-day waiting period was being applied uniformly across all facilities --- but we had no documentation to confirm whether this waiting period was consistent with the requirements of New York law or whether it should have varied by state. These errors are not catastrophic individually, but they indicate systemic weaknesses that will only worsen as the Company scales into additional jurisdictions.

**Recommendation.** I strongly recommend that the Company invest in a dedicated leave management software solution that integrates with WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync Enterprise and can be configured with state-specific rules for all seven jurisdictions. Based on preliminary research, I estimate that implementation of such a system --- including licensing fees, configuration, data migration, and staff training --- would cost between \$40,000 and \$75,000 and would require approximately three to six months to fully implement. I recognize that this represents a significant portion of the \$175,000 compliance remediation budget that has been allocated for legal fees, system updates, and policy redrafting, and I address this further in Section 8 below. Without a system upgrade, the Company faces significant compliance risk from miscalculated benefits, missed notice obligations, and inadequate recordkeeping --- particularly given that several of the expansion states impose specific recordkeeping requirements on employers regarding leave accruals and usage.

**[5.2 HRIS and Time & Attendance Considerations]{.underline}**

**WorkBridge Pro.** WorkBridge Pro Version 8.4 supports multi-state configurations, but the system has not been set up for Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, or Washington. My team will need to create new organizational units, cost centers, and location codes for each expansion facility, as well as configure state-specific tax and deduction profiles. Based on past experience with system configuration, I estimate this effort will require approximately two to three weeks of dedicated configuration time per state, plus testing and validation. This work should be sequenced to align with each facility\'s opening timeline.

**ClockSync Enterprise.** ClockSync Enterprise will need to be deployed at each new warehouse and distribution facility for hourly employee time and attendance tracking. This requires procurement and installation of physical time clock hardware at each site, network configuration, and enrollment of employee biometric or badge credentials. Based on vendor lead times for hardware procurement and on-site installation, I estimate approximately four to six weeks of lead time per facility. I recommend initiating procurement for the Worcester, Massachusetts facility immediately, given the August 1, 2025 opening date.

**Keystone Benefit Administrators.** Keystone will need to extend the voluntary short-term disability insurance offering to employees in the expansion states. I will coordinate with Keystone to determine whether any state-specific regulatory approvals or product filings are required. This is a lower-priority item relative to the mandatory paid leave compliance issues, but it should not be overlooked.

**[5.3 HR Staffing]{.underline}**

My HR team currently consists of 11 people, supporting 2,140 employees across three states and five facilities. Adding 420 employees in four new states and four new facilities will meaningfully strain the team\'s capacity. The expansion does not merely increase headcount --- it introduces regulatory complexity across multiple new jurisdictions, each with its own employment laws, leave requirements, and administrative obligations.

I recommend hiring at least two additional HR generalists: one dedicated to the New England and East Coast expansion (primarily Massachusetts, with support for existing Connecticut operations) and one for the Western expansion (Colorado, Oregon, and Washington). I also recommend designating a leave administration specialist within the team whose primary responsibility will be managing the increasingly complex multi-state leave landscape, whether through the current manual spreadsheet process or, preferably, through a newly implemented leave management software system. This role could be filled by reassigning an existing team member or by hiring a new specialist, depending on budget availability.

**[6. Known Compliance Concerns and Pending Matters]{.underline}**

**[6.1 NJ DOL Complaint (Carla Muñoz)]{.underline}**

For background context, I want to note a pending matter that has heightened the urgency of the broader leave policy review. On February 3, 2025, the Company received a complaint from the New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development, assigned Case Number NJ-ESL-2025-04117, filed by Carla Muñoz. Ms. Muñoz is a warehouse associate at the Edison, New Jersey facility. The complaint alleges that she was denied the right to use earned sick leave to care for her domestic partner\'s child. The Company\'s response to the complaint was filed on February 21, 2025 by Hartwell & Cabot LLP, the outside employment law firm that the Company has used for discrete matters. The matter remains open and under review by the Department.

This complaint has highlighted the need to review the Company\'s leave policies more broadly. Specifically, the family member definition in our current sick leave policy may be narrower than what New Jersey law --- and the laws of other states --- requires. I flag this as a key item for outside counsel\'s comprehensive review, as the definition of covered family members is a recurring issue in multi-state leave compliance.

**[6.2 Employee Handbook --- Last Updated January 15, 2022]{.underline}**

I want to draw attention to the fact that the Company\'s Employee Handbook, including all leave policies (paid sick leave, paid family leave, paid medical leave/short-term disability, and PTO/vacation), was last updated on January 15, 2022. The policies contained in the current Handbook were designed primarily to comply with New Jersey and New York requirements, with only informal consideration given to Connecticut at the time the Handbook was drafted. No substantive updates have been made since January 2022.

With the expansion into four new states --- each with its own paid leave laws, accrual requirements, and employer obligations --- the leave policies will require significant revision. I recommend targeting July 1, 2025 as the publication date for a revised Employee Handbook. That date is approximately one month before the first expansion facility (Worcester, Massachusetts) opens on August 1, 2025, and would allow time for employee distribution and acknowledgment collection before the first new-state employees are onboarded.

**[6.3 Self-Funded Leave Approach]{.underline}**

I want to briefly flag an issue that I believe warrants review by outside counsel. The Company currently self-funds its paid family leave benefit, providing eligible employees with 50% of their average weekly wage, capped at \$850 per week, for up to eight weeks. This self-funded benefit was established as an alternative to participation in state-administered insurance programs.

I have received questions from employees in Connecticut who have asked about the Connecticut Paid Leave program and whether the Company participates. Several employees have expressed confusion about whether they are covered by the state program or by the Company\'s self-funded benefit, and at least two employees have asked whether the Company\'s benefit provides the same level of coverage as the state program. I defer to Legal on whether the Company\'s self-funded approach satisfies the requirements of each state\'s paid leave law, or whether the Company should be participating in the applicable state insurance programs --- whether mandatorily or optionally. I ask that this question be included in outside counsel\'s scope of review.

**[7. Recommended Action Items and Timeline]{.underline}**

Based on the analysis set forth in this memorandum, I have prepared the following consolidated list of recommended action items with proposed deadlines. I present these in chronological order and identify proposed owners for each item.

**1. IMMEDIATE --- April 2025**

> • Determine Colorado FAMLI registration requirements for the three remote employees starting in April 2025. Clarify whether the Company\'s registration obligation is already overdue as of the first date of hire for a Colorado-based employee, regardless of whether a physical facility is operational. *Owner: Legal (Rebecca Thorsen), with HR support.*
>
> • Engage outside employment counsel to conduct a comprehensive multi-state compliance review. I understand that Rebecca is planning to retain Hartwell & Cabot LLP for this purpose, and this memorandum is intended to provide the operational context that will support that engagement. *Owner: Rebecca Thorsen.*

**2. BY MID-MAY 2025**

> • Complete Massachusetts PFML employer registration with the Massachusetts Department of Family and Medical Leave. This must be completed in time to allow PinnacleHR six to eight weeks to configure withholding before the August 1, 2025 facility opening in Worcester. Mid-May is the latest acceptable completion date. *Owner: HR (Marcus Delgado) and Legal, with PinnacleHR coordination by Priya Samuelson.*
>
> • Receive compliance matrix and gap analysis from outside counsel. Based on my understanding of the anticipated engagement timeline, deliverables should be targeted for approximately mid-to-late May 2025. *Owner: Outside counsel / Rebecca Thorsen.*

**3. BY JULY 1, 2025**

> • Publish a revised Employee Handbook incorporating multi-state leave policy updates for all seven jurisdictions (NJ, NY, CT, MA, CO, OR, WA). The revised Handbook should address paid sick leave, paid family leave, paid medical leave, PTO, and all applicable state-specific requirements. *Owner: Marcus Delgado, with outside counsel input and Legal approval.*
>
> • Begin formal evaluation and selection of a dedicated leave management software solution to replace the current spreadsheet-based tracking system. Issue an RFP or conduct vendor demonstrations by this date. *Owner: Marcus Delgado, with IT support.*

**4. BY AUGUST 2025**

> • Complete Colorado FAMLI employer registration if not already completed for the three early remote hires. Ensure PinnacleHR configuration for Colorado is complete. *Owner: HR / Legal.*
>
> • **Massachusetts facility goes live August 1, 2025.** All leave-related systems, policies, payroll configurations, and registrations must be fully operational by this date.

**5. BY OCTOBER--NOVEMBER 2025**

> • Complete Paid Leave Oregon employer registration. Allow PinnacleHR six to eight weeks of configuration lead time ahead of the February 1, 2026 facility opening. *Owner: HR / Legal.*
>
> • **Colorado facility goes live November 1, 2025.** All Colorado systems and registrations must be fully operational by this date.

**6. BY DECEMBER 2025--JANUARY 2026**

> • Complete Washington PFML employer registration. Allow PinnacleHR configuration time ahead of the May 1, 2026 facility opening. *Owner: HR / Legal.*
>
> • Implement dedicated leave management software. Target: system live and fully configured for all seven states by Q1 2026, in time to support the Oregon and Washington facility launches. *Owner: Marcus Delgado, with IT support and vendor coordination.*

**7. BY FEBRUARY 2026**

> • **Oregon facility goes live February 1, 2026.** All Oregon systems and registrations must be fully operational by this date.

**8. BY MAY 2026**

> • **Washington facility goes live May 1, 2026.** All Washington systems and registrations must be fully operational by this date.
>
> • All four expansion states fully onboarded to payroll, leave management, and HRIS systems. Post-launch audit of all systems and compliance processes to be conducted within 30 days of the Washington opening.

**[8. Budget Considerations]{.underline}**

The following anticipated costs are associated with the HR and compliance workstream of the expansion. I present these for Rebecca\'s awareness and to support budget allocation discussions with Finance.

**Outside Counsel Fees (Hartwell & Cabot LLP).** The Company has allocated a compliance remediation budget of \$175,000 to cover legal fees, system updates, and policy redrafting. Based on my understanding, Hartwell & Cabot\'s billing rates are \$475 per hour for partner time and \$340 per hour for senior associate time. The scope of the anticipated multi-state compliance review, policy drafting, and registration assistance will consume a meaningful portion of this budget. I do not have a specific estimate from the firm but recommend that Rebecca obtain a fee estimate or budget cap as part of the engagement letter.

**Leave Management Software.** As discussed in Section 5.1, I estimate the cost of licensing, configuring, and deploying a dedicated leave management software solution at \$40,000 to \$75,000. This estimate includes licensing fees, state-specific rule configuration, integration with WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync Enterprise, data migration, and staff training. If this expenditure is drawn from the \$175,000 compliance remediation budget, it will significantly reduce the funds available for outside counsel fees and other compliance activities.

**Additional HR Headcount.** I recommend hiring two additional HR generalists, at an estimated combined annual salary cost of \$120,000 to \$140,000. This cost would not be drawn from the compliance remediation budget but would need to be approved as a separate headcount and compensation expenditure through the normal budgeting process.

**PinnacleHR Configuration Fees.** Priya Samuelson has indicated that configuration of payroll and withholding for new states may be covered under the Company\'s existing service agreement with PinnacleHR. I will verify this and report back. If additional fees are required, they are expected to be modest.

**WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync Enterprise Deployment.** Configuration of WorkBridge Pro for the four new states and procurement and installation of ClockSync Enterprise hardware at the four new facilities is estimated at \$15,000 to \$25,000 in total. This includes hardware costs, on-site installation, network configuration, and system testing.

I note that the \$175,000 compliance remediation budget may be tight if a significant leave management software investment is required simultaneously with a comprehensive outside counsel engagement. I recommend that Rebecca confirm budget allocation priorities with Finance and determine whether additional funding can be secured for the technology investment, or whether the compliance remediation budget should be reserved primarily for legal fees with the software investment pursued through a separate capital expenditure request.

**[9. Conclusion]{.underline}**

The four-state expansion into Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington presents meaningful HR infrastructure and compliance challenges that must be addressed proactively and on a compressed timeline. While the business case for the expansion is sound and the operational planning is well advanced, the HR and compliance workstreams require immediate attention to avoid regulatory exposure and operational disruption.

I consider the following three items to be the most urgent priorities:

1\. **State paid leave program registrations for Colorado and Massachusetts.** As of the date of this memorandum, the Company has not registered with any of the four expansion states\' paid leave insurance programs. Colorado is the most urgent due to three remote employees being hired in April 2025, and Massachusetts is on the critical path given the August 1, 2025 facility opening and PinnacleHR\'s six-to-eight-week configuration lead time.

2\. **Replacing the manual spreadsheet-based leave tracking system with scalable software.** The current system is inadequate for seven-state operations and poses compliance and recordkeeping risks. A dedicated leave management solution should be selected and implemented before the Oregon and Washington facilities open in 2026.

3\. **Revising the Employee Handbook leave policies for multi-state compliance.** The current Handbook was last updated on January 15, 2022 and does not address the requirements of the four expansion states. A revised Handbook should be published by July 1, 2025.

I respectfully request a meeting with Rebecca at her earliest convenience to discuss priorities, budget allocation, and next steps. I am prepared to provide any additional materials, data, or analysis that may be needed to support the anticipated engagement of outside counsel, and I will make myself and my team fully available to assist with that review.

Marcus Delgado Director of Human Resources Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300 Edison, NJ 08817

**[SECTION 6: LEAVE POLICIES]{.underline}**

**[6.1 General Leave Policy Overview]{.underline}**

Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (\"the Company\" or \"Vantage\") is committed to providing competitive leave benefits to its employees while maintaining compliance with applicable federal and state laws. The Company recognizes that employees may need time away from work for a variety of personal, medical, and family-related reasons, and the leave policies described in this Section are designed to support those needs in a structured and equitable manner.

The Company currently operates facilities in the following locations:

> • **Edison, New Jersey** (Corporate Headquarters and Warehouse) --- 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, NJ 08817
>
> • **Newark, New Jersey** (Distribution Center) --- 1275 Raymond Boulevard, Building C, Newark, NJ 07102
>
> • **Maspeth, Queens, New York** (Warehouse) --- 56-10 Grand Avenue, Maspeth, NY 11378
>
> • **White Plains, New York** (Satellite Office) --- 200 Hamilton Avenue, Suite 410, White Plains, NY 10601
>

As of the date of this revision, the Company employs approximately 2,140 employees across these five facilities, including full-time, part-time, temporary, and seasonal workers in a range of administrative, operational, logistics, and management roles.

The policies set forth in this Section 6 apply uniformly to all Company employees regardless of work location, unless a specific provision expressly states otherwise. The Company reserves the right to amend, modify, or terminate any of the leave policies described herein at any time, with or without notice, subject to applicable law. Where state law provides greater benefits than those described herein, the Company will comply with applicable state law.

Employees who have questions regarding any of the leave policies described in this Section, or who require assistance in understanding their leave entitlements, should direct their inquiries to the Human Resources Department. The Human Resources Department is led by Marcus Delgado, Director of Human Resources, who may be reached at the Company\'s principal office in Edison, New Jersey, by telephone at extension 4200, or by email at hr@vantagelogistics.com.

Leave accruals, usage, and balances are tracked through the Company\'s internal Human Resources records. Payroll, including the processing of leave-related payments and deductions, is administered by PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc., the Company\'s third-party payroll vendor. Hourly employee time and attendance is recorded using ClockSync Enterprise, an integrated time-tracking system installed at all Company facilities. All employee records, including leave records, are maintained in WorkBridge Pro (Version 8.4), the Company\'s Human Resources Information System (HRIS) platform. Employees should ensure that their time entries are accurate and complete, as leave accruals are calculated based on recorded hours. Any discrepancies in time records should be reported to the employee\'s supervisor and the Human Resources Department promptly.

**[6.2 Paid Sick Leave Policy]{.underline}**

**6.2.1 Eligibility.** All employees of Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. are eligible to accrue paid sick leave, regardless of classification as full-time, part-time, temporary, or seasonal. There are no minimum tenure requirements for the accrual of paid sick leave. Accrual begins on the employee\'s date of hire and continues for the duration of employment with the Company. Eligibility for accrual is not conditioned upon completion of a probationary or introductory employment period.

**6.2.2 Accrual Rate**

Employees accrue paid sick leave at a rate of one (1) hour for every forty (40) hours worked. For hourly (non-exempt) employees, accrual is calculated based on actual hours worked as recorded in ClockSync Enterprise. Overtime hours are included in the calculation of hours worked for accrual purposes. For salaried (exempt) employees, accrual is calculated based on a standard forty (40)-hour workweek, regardless of the actual number of hours worked in a given week. Accrued sick leave balances are reflected on employees\' pay stubs processed by PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. Employees are encouraged to review their pay stubs regularly to confirm the accuracy of their sick leave balances.

**6.2.3 Maximum Accrual Cap**

The maximum amount of paid sick leave that an employee may accrue in a single calendar year is determined by the employee\'s classification as follows:

**Full-Time Employees.** Full-time employees (defined as employees regularly scheduled to work thirty (30) or more hours per week) may accrue a maximum of forty (40) hours of paid sick leave per calendar year. Once the forty (40)-hour cap is reached, no additional sick leave will accrue until the start of the next calendar year. For purposes of this policy, a \"calendar year\" means the twelve (12)-month period beginning on January 1 and ending on December 31.

**Part-Time Employees.** Part-time employees (defined as employees regularly scheduled for fewer than thirty (30) hours per week) may accrue a maximum of twenty-four (24) hours of paid sick leave per calendar year. Once the twenty-four (24)-hour cap is reached, no additional sick leave will accrue until the start of the next calendar year.

The accrual caps set forth above apply regardless of the number of hours actually worked during the calendar year. No employee may accrue paid sick leave in excess of the applicable cap, and the Company will not credit additional sick leave hours once the cap has been reached. Temporary and seasonal employees are subject to the accrual cap corresponding to their scheduled hours per week at the time of hire or most recent schedule change.

**6.2.4 Waiting Period Before Use**

Employees may begin using accrued paid sick leave on the one hundred twentieth (120th) calendar day following their date of hire. Sick leave used prior to the 120th day of employment will not be compensated. This waiting period is a uniform, company-wide policy applicable to all employees at all Company facilities, regardless of the state in which the employee is employed. The waiting period does not affect the accrual of sick leave; employees continue to accrue sick leave from their date of hire during the waiting period. Once the 120-day waiting period has elapsed, the employee may use any and all sick leave accrued to that point, subject to the terms and conditions of this policy.

**6.2.5 Permitted Uses of Paid Sick Leave**

Paid sick leave may be used for the following purposes:

> \(a\) The employee\'s own illness, injury, or medical condition requiring the absence of the employee from work, including but not limited to physical illness, mental health conditions, and recovery from surgery or medical procedures;
>
> \(b\) The employee\'s own medical appointment, including routine examination or diagnostic testing, that cannot be reasonably scheduled outside of working hours. Employees are expected to make reasonable efforts to schedule medical appointments during non-working hours or at times that minimize disruption to operations; and
>
> \(c\) The employee\'s own injury sustained while performing work duties, to the extent that the use of sick leave is appropriate in coordination with any applicable workers\' compensation leave. Employees who are injured on the job should also follow the Company\'s workers\' compensation reporting procedures as set forth in Section 9.5 of this Handbook.

Paid sick leave is intended for the employee\'s own health-related needs as described above. Sick leave may be used in increments of no less than one (1) hour. Employees who wish to use sick leave should notify their supervisor as soon as practicable, and in no event later than the start of the employee\'s scheduled shift on the day of the absence, unless the employee is physically unable to provide such notice due to the nature of the illness or injury. The Company reserves the right to deny sick leave requests that do not fall within the permitted uses described in this section.

**6.2.6 Definition of Family Member (for Sick Leave Purposes)**

To the extent that any applicable law requires the Company to permit sick leave for the care of a family member, \"family member\" is defined as follows for purposes of this Section 6.2:

> \(i\) the employee\'s spouse (including a same-sex spouse in a legally recognized marriage);
>
> \(ii\) the employee\'s child, including a biological child, adopted child, foster child, or stepchild, regardless of age; and
>
> \(iii\) the employee\'s parent, including a biological parent, adoptive parent, foster parent, or step-parent.

For the avoidance of doubt, \"family member\" does not include individuals outside of the foregoing categories unless required by applicable law on a case-by-case basis. The Company believes that the definition set forth above encompasses the core family relationships recognized under the majority of applicable leave statutes. Employees who believe that their specific family situation requires consideration beyond this definition should contact the Human Resources Department, which will review the matter in light of applicable law and respond in a timely manner. Nothing in this subsection shall be construed to expand the permitted uses of sick leave set forth in Section 6.2.5 above.

**6.2.7 Carryover of Unused Sick Leave**

Unused paid sick leave does NOT carry over from one calendar year to the next. On January 1 of each year, all employees\' sick leave balances will reset to zero (0) hours, and accrual will begin anew for the new calendar year. The Company does not frontload sick leave at the beginning of the calendar year; all sick leave must be earned through actual hours worked in accordance with the accrual rate described in Section 6.2.2.

Employees are encouraged to use their accrued sick leave during the calendar year in which it is earned. The Company will not compensate employees for unused sick leave at the end of the calendar year or at any other time. Employees should plan their use of sick leave accordingly and should not rely on the availability of unused sick leave in future calendar years. The annual reset of sick leave balances will be reflected on the first pay stub of each calendar year.

**6.2.8 Medical Documentation Requirements**

For any absence due to paid sick leave exceeding two (2) consecutive scheduled workdays, the employee must provide a signed note from a licensed healthcare provider confirming the necessity of the absence. The documentation must include the date(s) of treatment or examination, a general statement that the employee was unable to work due to a medical condition, and the expected date of return. The healthcare provider\'s note need not specify the particular diagnosis or nature of the medical condition.

The documentation must be submitted to the Human Resources Department within three (3) business days of the employee\'s return to work. Failure to provide required documentation within this timeframe may result in the absence being reclassified as unexcused and may be subject to disciplinary action under the Company\'s attendance policy (see Section 9.3 of this Handbook). Disciplinary action for unexcused absences may include verbal warning, written warning, suspension, or termination, depending on the circumstances and the employee\'s prior disciplinary history.

The Company reserves the right to request documentation for absences of any duration if a pattern of abuse is suspected, subject to applicable law. A \"pattern of abuse\" may include, but is not limited to, repeated absences on Mondays or Fridays, absences adjacent to holidays or scheduled vacation, or a sudden increase in the frequency of sick leave usage without apparent explanation.

**6.2.9 No Payout Upon Separation**

Unused accrued paid sick leave will not be paid out upon an employee\'s separation from employment, whether voluntary or involuntary, under any circumstances. This policy applies regardless of the employee\'s length of service, the reason for separation, or the amount of unused sick leave in the employee\'s account at the time of separation.

**6.2.10 Anti-Retaliation Statement**

The Company will not retaliate against any employee for requesting or using paid sick leave in accordance with this policy and applicable law. Retaliation for the lawful use of sick leave is strictly prohibited and will result in disciplinary action against the retaliating party. Employees who believe they have experienced retaliation for requesting or using sick leave should contact the Human Resources Department or report the matter through the Company\'s anonymous ethics hotline at 1-888-555-0142.

**[6.3 Paid Family Leave Policy]{.underline}**

**6.3.1 Purpose and Scope**

Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. provides paid family leave benefits to eligible employees to support bonding with a new child or caring for a family member with a serious health condition. The Company recognizes the importance of family and is committed to providing meaningful support to employees during significant family events.

This benefit is funded entirely by the Company. The Company self-funds its paid family leave benefit and does not participate in state paid family leave insurance programs. The Company believes that its self-funded paid family leave benefit meets or exceeds the requirements of applicable state paid family leave programs in the jurisdictions in which the Company currently operates. The Company\'s decision to self-fund this benefit reflects its commitment to providing a streamlined, consistent leave experience for all employees, regardless of location, without requiring employees to navigate separate state insurance claim processes.

Employees should refer to this Section 6.3 for complete details regarding eligibility, qualifying reasons, duration, wage replacement, and other terms and conditions of the Company\'s paid family leave benefit.

**6.3.2 Eligibility.** To be eligible for paid family leave under this policy, an employee must meet BOTH of the following requirements:

> \(a\) The employee must have been employed by the Company for at least twelve (12) consecutive months as of the date leave is requested; and
>
> \(b\) The employee must have worked at least one thousand (1,000) hours during the twelve (12)-month period immediately preceding the date leave is requested.

Hours worked are tracked by the Human Resources Department using Company records, including data from ClockSync Enterprise (for hourly employees) and WorkBridge Pro (for all employees). For purposes of determining eligibility, \"hours worked\" includes actual time worked, paid leave, and any other time counted as hours worked under applicable law, but does not include unpaid leave or absences.

Employees who do not meet these eligibility requirements may be eligible for unpaid leave under the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) or applicable state law. Employees who are uncertain about their eligibility for paid family leave should contact the Human Resources Department for an eligibility determination prior to submitting a formal leave request.

**6.3.3 Qualifying Reasons for Leave**

Paid family leave may be taken for the following qualifying reasons only:

> \(a\) **Bonding with a newborn child.** Leave for bonding with a newborn must be taken within twelve (12) months of the child\'s date of birth. Both parents are eligible for bonding leave, provided each meets the eligibility requirements set forth in Section 6.3.2.
>
> \(b\) **Bonding with a child newly placed for adoption or foster care.** Leave for bonding with a child newly placed with the employee for adoption or foster care must be taken within twelve (12) months of the date of placement.
>
> \(c\) **Caring for a family member with a serious health condition.** Leave may be taken to provide care for a family member (as defined below) who has a serious health condition that requires the employee\'s presence and assistance.

For purposes of this Section 6.3, a \"serious health condition\" shall have the meaning set forth in the Family and Medical Leave Act, 29 U.S.C. § 2611(11), and its implementing regulations: a condition that requires inpatient care in a hospital, hospice, or residential medical care facility, or a condition that requires continuing treatment by a health care provider. The determination of whether a condition constitutes a \"serious health condition\" shall be made by the employee\'s or family member\'s health care provider and is subject to verification by the Company in accordance with applicable law.

For purposes of this Section 6.3, \"family member\" is defined as:

> \(i\) the employee\'s spouse (including a same-sex spouse in a legally recognized marriage);
>
> \(ii\) the employee\'s child, including a biological child, adopted child, foster child, or stepchild, or a child for whom the employee stands in loco parentis, regardless of age; and
>
> \(iii\) the employee\'s parent, including a biological parent, adoptive parent, foster parent, or step-parent. \"Parent\" does not include parents-in-law.

The qualifying reasons set forth above are the exclusive qualifying reasons for paid family leave under this policy. Leave for any other purpose, including the employee\'s own serious health condition or disability, is not covered under this Section 6.3 and should be addressed under Sections 6.2 (Paid Sick Leave), 6.4 (Paid Medical Leave / Short-Term Disability), or 6.5 (Paid Time Off) as applicable.

**6.3.4 Duration of Leave**

Eligible employees may take a maximum of eight (8) weeks of paid family leave in any twelve (12)-month period. For purposes of this policy, the twelve (12)-month period is measured on a rolling backward basis from the date leave is used. This means that each time an employee takes paid family leave, the remaining leave entitlement is calculated by subtracting the amount of paid family leave already taken during the immediately preceding twelve (12) months.

Paid family leave may be taken on a continuous basis. Alternatively, with the prior written approval of the employee\'s supervisor, paid family leave may be taken on an intermittent basis in full-day increments only. Intermittent leave is not available in increments of less than one (1) full workday. Requests for intermittent leave are subject to operational needs and may be denied if the intermittent schedule would cause undue disruption to the Company\'s operations.

Paid family leave under this policy runs concurrently with any FMLA leave for which the employee is eligible. Employees should consult with the Human Resources Department regarding the interaction between the Company\'s paid family leave benefit and any FMLA entitlements.

**6.3.5 Wage Replacement and Payment**

During paid family leave, the employee will receive fifty percent (50%) of the employee\'s average weekly wage, calculated based on the employee\'s earnings during the eight (8) weeks immediately preceding the start of leave. For purposes of this calculation, \"earnings\" include the employee\'s regular base pay and do not include overtime, bonuses, commissions, or other supplemental compensation.

The maximum weekly benefit under this policy is Eight Hundred Fifty Dollars (\$850.00) per week, regardless of the employee\'s average weekly wage. An employee whose fifty percent (50%) wage replacement calculation would exceed \$850.00 per week will receive the capped amount of \$850.00 per week.

Payment will be made on the Company\'s regular payroll schedule, processed through PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. Leave payments are subject to all applicable federal, state, and local tax withholdings and deductions. This benefit is funded entirely by the Company; no employee contributions are required or deducted for the purpose of funding this paid family leave benefit.

Employees who wish to supplement their paid family leave payments with accrued PTO to bring their total weekly compensation closer to their regular wages may do so, subject to the provisions of Section 6.5 and the coordination rules in Section 6.3.7. In no event may an employee\'s combined compensation from paid family leave and PTO supplementation exceed one hundred percent (100%) of the employee\'s regular weekly wages.

**6.3.6 Notice Requirements**

Employees requesting paid family leave must provide at least thirty (30) calendar days\' advance written notice to their supervisor and the Human Resources Department when the need for leave is foreseeable. A need for leave is considered \"foreseeable\" when the employee has advance knowledge of the need, such as an expected due date for the birth of a child, a planned adoption or foster care placement date, or a scheduled medical procedure for a family member.

Notice must be submitted using the Company\'s Leave Request Form, which is available from the Human Resources Department or on the Company intranet. The Leave Request Form must include the anticipated start date of the leave, the expected duration of the leave, and the qualifying reason for the leave, including supporting details sufficient to allow the Company to determine eligibility. A sample Leave Request Form is attached as Appendix B to this Section.

If thirty (30) calendar days\' advance notice is not practicable due to circumstances beyond the employee\'s control, the employee must provide notice as far in advance as possible. In all cases, the employee should make reasonable efforts to notify their supervisor and the Human Resources Department before the leave begins. Failure to provide adequate notice may delay the start of paid family leave or result in the initial period of absence being treated as unpaid leave, subject to applicable law.

The Human Resources Department will review all leave requests and notify the employee of the approval or denial of the request within five (5) business days of receipt. If additional documentation or information is required, the Human Resources Department will notify the employee in writing.

**6.3.7 Coordination with Other Leave**

Paid family leave under this policy runs concurrently with FMLA leave, where the employee is eligible for FMLA leave. Employees may not \"stack\" paid family leave on top of FMLA leave to extend the total duration of job-protected leave beyond the amount provided under the FMLA or this policy, whichever is greater.

Employees may supplement paid family leave payments with accrued PTO to reach full salary replacement, provided that the employee\'s combined compensation from paid family leave and PTO does not exceed one hundred percent (100%) of the employee\'s regular weekly wages. Employees who wish to supplement their leave in this manner should indicate their intention on the Leave Request Form.

Paid family leave under this policy is not available in addition to any state-mandated paid family leave benefit for the same qualifying event. The Company\'s self-funded paid family leave benefit is intended to satisfy any applicable state mandate for paid family leave. Employees who have questions about the interaction of the Company\'s benefit with state programs should contact the Human Resources Department for guidance.

**6.3.8 Job Protection and Benefits Continuation**

Employees on approved paid family leave will continue to receive Company-provided health insurance benefits on the same terms and conditions as if the employee were actively employed. The employee\'s share of any premium contribution will continue to be deducted from the employee\'s leave payments. If leave payments are insufficient to cover the employee\'s premium share, the employee will be responsible for remitting the difference to the Human Resources Department on a monthly basis.

Upon return from approved paid family leave, the Company will restore the employee to the same position held prior to the commencement of leave or to an equivalent position with equivalent pay, benefits, and other terms and conditions of employment. Reinstatement is subject to the same limitations applicable to reinstatement from FMLA leave, including the exception for \"key employees\" as defined under the FMLA.

The Company strictly prohibits retaliation against any employee for requesting or taking paid family leave under this policy. Employees who believe they have been subjected to retaliation should immediately report the matter to the Human Resources Department, the Company\'s General Counsel, or through the Company\'s anonymous ethics hotline.

**[6.4 Paid Medical Leave / Short-Term Disability]{.underline}**

**6.4.1 Company-Provided Paid Medical Leave**

The Company does NOT provide employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee\'s own serious health condition or disability beyond the accrued sick leave and PTO benefits described in Sections 6.2 and 6.5 of this Handbook. Employees who are unable to work due to their own serious health condition, illness, injury, or disability should first use any available accrued sick leave and, if applicable, accrued PTO.

Employees who exhaust their accrued sick leave and PTO while on medical leave will be placed on unpaid leave status. Eligibility for job-protected unpaid leave is governed by the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) and applicable state law. Employees seeking unpaid FMLA leave should submit a request to the Human Resources Department, which will determine eligibility and provide the employee with the required FMLA notices and certification forms.

**6.4.2 Voluntary Short-Term Disability Insurance**

The Company offers voluntary short-term disability (STD) insurance through Keystone Benefit Administrators. This benefit is available to all eligible employees on a voluntary, employee-paid basis. Enrollment in the voluntary STD plan is entirely optional and is not a condition of employment.

Coverage details, including benefit levels, elimination periods, benefit duration, and enrollment procedures, are available from the Human Resources Department or through Keystone Benefit Administrators directly. Employees interested in enrolling in the voluntary STD plan should contact the Human Resources Department to obtain enrollment materials and information regarding applicable enrollment windows.

Enrollment in the voluntary STD plan is at the employee\'s sole expense. The Company does not contribute to the cost of this benefit. Premium payments for the voluntary STD plan are deducted from the employee\'s wages on a post-tax basis through PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc.

The voluntary STD plan is a supplemental benefit and does not replace or substitute for any state-mandated disability or medical leave insurance program. Employees who are enrolled in the voluntary STD plan and who also qualify for benefits under a state-mandated disability or medical leave program may be eligible to coordinate benefits from both sources, subject to the terms of the STD plan and applicable state law.

**6.4.3 State-Mandated Disability / Medical Leave Programs**

Employees in certain states may be eligible for state-mandated temporary disability or medical leave benefits. The Company participates in applicable state programs as required by law. Employee contributions, where required by state law, are deducted from employee wages and remitted by PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. on the employee\'s behalf in accordance with applicable state requirements.

For questions regarding state-mandated disability or medical leave benefits, including eligibility, benefit levels, claim filing procedures, and coordination with other benefits, please contact the Human Resources Department.

**[6.5 Paid Time Off (PTO) / Vacation Policy]{.underline}**

**6.5.1 Eligibility and Accrual Schedule**

All full-time employees of the Company are eligible for paid time off (PTO) based on their length of continuous service with Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. PTO accrues on a bi-weekly basis, with each pay period\'s accrual calculated as the employee\'s annual PTO entitlement divided by twenty-six (26) pay periods. PTO begins accruing on the employee\'s date of hire.

The following accrual schedule applies to all full-time employees:

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Length of Continuous Service**   **Annual PTO Entitlement**   **Bi-Weekly Accrual Rate**
  ---------------------------------- ---------------------------- ----------------------------
  0 through 2 years                  10 days (80 hours)           3.08 hours per pay period

  3 through 7 years                  15 days (120 hours)          4.62 hours per pay period

  8 or more years                    20 days (160 hours)          6.15 hours per pay period
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Part-time employees (defined as employees regularly scheduled for fewer than thirty (30) hours per week) receive PTO on a pro-rated basis, calculated in proportion to their regularly scheduled hours relative to a standard forty (40)-hour workweek. PTO accrual adjustments based on changes in length of service will take effect at the beginning of the pay period following the employee\'s service anniversary date.

**6.5.2 Use of PTO**

PTO may be used for vacation, personal appointments, personal business, or any other purpose at the employee\'s discretion. Employees are not required to disclose the reason for using PTO. Employees must request PTO through their supervisor at least two (2) weeks in advance for planned absences of three (3) or more consecutive workdays, and at least forty-eight (48) hours in advance for shorter planned absences. PTO requests are subject to supervisor approval and may be denied or rescheduled based on operational needs, staffing requirements, or other business considerations. The Company will make reasonable efforts to accommodate PTO requests but cannot guarantee approval of all requests, particularly during peak operational periods.

**6.5.3 Maximum Carryover**

Employees may carry over a maximum of five (5) days (forty (40) hours) of unused PTO from one calendar year to the next. Any unused PTO in excess of five (5) days as of December 31 of each year will be forfeited. Forfeited PTO will not be compensated. Carryover PTO must be used by March 31 of the following year or it will be forfeited. The Company encourages employees to use their PTO during the year in which it is earned and to plan their time off to avoid forfeiture of unused PTO at year end. It is the employee\'s responsibility to monitor their PTO balance and schedule time off accordingly.

**6.5.4 PTO Payout Upon Separation**

Upon voluntary or involuntary separation from employment, the Company does NOT pay out accrued, unused PTO, except where required by applicable state law. Currently, employees based in Connecticut will receive payout of accrued, unused PTO upon separation from employment, in accordance with Connecticut state law. The payout amount will be calculated based on the employee\'s regular rate of pay at the time of separation and will be included in the employee\'s final paycheck.

In all other jurisdictions in which the Company currently operates, unused PTO is forfeited upon separation from employment. Employees are encouraged to plan their PTO usage accordingly and to use accrued PTO prior to any anticipated separation. The Company will not make exceptions to this policy except as required by applicable law.

**6.5.5 PTO and Other Leave**

PTO may be used to supplement paid family leave payments to bring the employee\'s total weekly compensation to one hundred percent (100%) of the employee\'s regular wages, as described in Section 6.3.7. PTO may also be used during any waiting period or elimination period applicable to voluntary short-term disability benefits through Keystone Benefit Administrators.

PTO usage does not reduce the employee\'s accrued sick leave balance, and sick leave usage does not reduce the employee\'s accrued PTO balance. The two programs operate independently. Employees may not use PTO and sick leave for the same absence on the same workday.

**[6.6 General Leave Administration]{.underline}**

**6.6.1 Leave Requests and Recordkeeping**

All leave requests must be submitted to the employee\'s direct supervisor and the Human Resources Department using the Company\'s Leave Request Form, which is available in both paper and electronic format. Paper forms may be obtained from the Human Resources Department at any Company facility. Electronic forms are available on the Company intranet. A sample Leave Request Form is attached as Appendix B to this Section.

The Human Resources Department is responsible for tracking all leave accruals, usage, and balances for every employee. Leave records are maintained by the Human Resources Department in accordance with the Company\'s recordkeeping procedures and applicable federal and state record retention requirements. The Company retains leave records for a minimum of six (6) years following the end of the calendar year to which the records relate.

Employees may request their current leave balances at any time by contacting the Human Resources Department. Balances for sick leave and PTO accruals are also reflected on payroll statements issued by PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. Employees are encouraged to review their payroll statements regularly and to report any discrepancies to the Human Resources Department within thirty (30) days of receipt of the applicable payroll statement.

**6.6.2 Interaction with Federal and State Leave Laws**

The Company\'s leave policies are intended to comply with all applicable federal and state laws, including but not limited to the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA), the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, the New York Paid Sick Leave Law, the Connecticut Paid Sick Leave Act, and other applicable state statutes. The Company monitors legislative and regulatory developments in the jurisdictions in which it operates and will update its policies as necessary to maintain compliance.

Where the provisions of this Handbook differ from the requirements of applicable federal or state law, the Company will comply with the applicable law. In the event of a conflict between this Handbook and applicable law, the applicable law shall control. This policy does not create any rights beyond those provided by applicable law, and employees should not rely on the provisions of this Handbook as a substitute for their rights under applicable statutes.

Employees are encouraged to consult with the Human Resources Department regarding their rights under applicable leave laws. The Human Resources Department can provide general information about leave entitlements but is not able to provide legal advice. Employees who have specific legal questions regarding their leave rights may wish to consult with a private attorney at their own expense.

**6.6.3 Employer Contributions and Employee Withholdings**

Where required by state law, the Company will make employer contributions to applicable state leave insurance programs. Employee contributions, where required by applicable state law, will be deducted from employee wages and remitted by PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. on the employee\'s behalf in accordance with applicable statutes and regulations.

Employee contribution rates are determined by applicable state law and may change annually. Current contribution rates are available from the Human Resources Department or on PinnacleHR\'s employee self-service portal. Employees with questions about the amounts being withheld from their wages for state leave insurance programs should contact the Human Resources Department or PinnacleHR Solutions directly.

**6.6.4 Prohibition on Retaliation**

The Company strictly prohibits retaliation against any employee for exercising rights under this leave policy or any applicable leave law. Retaliation includes, but is not limited to, termination, demotion, suspension, reduction in hours, transfer to a less desirable position, denial of a promotion or raise, or any other adverse employment action taken because of an employee\'s request for or use of leave.

Any supervisor, manager, or other employee who engages in retaliatory conduct will be subject to disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment. Employees who believe they have been subjected to retaliation should immediately report the matter to the Human Resources Department, the Company\'s General Counsel (Rebecca Thorsen, Senior Vice President & General Counsel), or through the Company\'s anonymous ethics hotline at 1-888-555-0142. All reports of retaliation will be investigated promptly and thoroughly, and the Company will take appropriate corrective action where warranted.

**6.6.5 Policy Amendments**

The Company reserves the right to modify, amend, or terminate any leave policy described in this Handbook at any time, with or without notice, subject to applicable law. Amendments will be communicated to employees through reasonable means, which may include electronic communication, posting on the Company intranet, distribution of updated Handbook pages, or other methods deemed appropriate by the Human Resources Department.

The most current version of this Handbook is available from the Human Resources Department at any Company facility. This revision is effective as of January 15, 2022 and supersedes all prior versions of Section 6 of the Employee Handbook, including all prior leave policy memoranda, bulletins, and communications that may conflict with the terms set forth herein.
